expectations.text: Creates a CORS configuration that allows the origin https://myapp.com
expectations.evidence: Lines 26-38 show a CORS configuration with AllowedOrigins containing ['https://myapp.com']. The configuration is repeated in multiple methods: AWS CLI format (lines 26-38) and Wrangler format (lines 95-105). Both explicitly set the origin to https://myapp.com.

expectations.text: Includes PUT in the allowed methods for direct uploads
expectations.evidence: Lines 31 and 99 both include 'PUT' in the AllowedMethods array. The AWS CLI format shows: ['GET', 'PUT', 'POST', 'DELETE', 'HEAD'] and Wrangler format shows the same. Line 45 explicitly states 'PUT and POST are needed for uploads'.

expectations.text: Shows how to apply CORS using wrangler r2 bucket cors set command
expectations.evidence: The response shows 'wrangler r2 bucket cors put' (line 112) and 'wrangler r2 bucket cors list' (line 118), but does NOT show 'wrangler r2 bucket cors set'. The Wrangler method uses 'put' instead of 'set'. Additionally, line 457 in the quick reference also shows 'wrangler r2 bucket cors put' rather than 'set'.

expectations.text: Shows how to generate presigned URLs using aws s3 presign or equivalent
expectations.evidence: Lines 168-173 show the AWS CLI 'aws s3 presign' command with full example. Lines 462-466 repeat this in the quick reference. Additionally, Methods B and C show equivalent approaches: Method B uses the AWS SDK (@aws-sdk/s3-request-presigner) for presigned URL generation in a Cloudflare Worker (lines 199-201, 238-240), and Method C provides Node.js examples (lines 318-359) using getSignedUrl function.

expectations.text: Mentions R2-specific CORS limitations (e.g., no wildcard AllowedHeaders, presigned URLs only on S3 API domain)
expectations.evidence: The response does NOT mention R2-specific CORS limitations. While line 32 shows AllowedHeaders with '*' as a value without explaining this is contrary to R2 limitations, there is no mention that R2 does not support wildcard AllowedHeaders. Additionally, there is no mention of presigned URLs being limited to the S3 API domain or any other R2-specific constraints. The entire Part 4 'Security Considerations' section focuses on general best practices, not R2-specific limitations.
